docs.title: Xeno Bot Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to the Xeno Bot documentation site. This repository contains guides and reference material to help you get started and contribute.

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text: • Getting Started

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text:
This section contains the high-level API for plugin authors.

Core classes

  • Plugin: base class for plugins
  • Command: represents a command, with name, handler, and help
  • Context: runtime context passed to handlers containing message, user, and send() helper

Example: simple plugin

from xeno_bot.plugin import Plugin, Command

class EchoPlugin(Plugin):
    def setup(self):
        self.add_command(Command("echo", self.echo))

    def echo(self, ctx, *args):
        return " ".join(args)

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text: Keep release notes and changelog entries here.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
Xeno Bot supports prefix-based commands and slash/interaction commands depending on integrations.

Basic usage

!help        # show help
!ping        # respond with pong
!echo hello  # plugin echo replies with `hello`

Writing a command plugin

  1. Create a plugin module under plugins/.
  2. Register commands via the plugin API:
from xeno_bot.plugin import Command

def ping(ctx):
    return "pong"

commands = [Command(name="ping", handler=ping)]

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Xeno Bot is configured via a YAML file (config.yml) placed at the project root or specified with --config.

Example config.yml:

bot:
name: xeno
token: "YOUR_TOKEN"
prefix: "!"

logging:
level: info

plugins:
- name: echo
enabled: true

Configuration options - bot.name: display name - bot.token: authentication token for platform - bot.prefix: command prefix - plugins: list of plugin configs

docs.title: Contributing to Xeno Bot
docs.text: Thank you for helping improve the docs and project. This file explains the recommended workflow for proposing documentation and code changes, how to run the docs locally, and how to get help.

docs.title: Suggested workflow
docs.text:
• Use the Edit this page link on any documentation page to propose documentation changes. That opens the source file on GitHub — make an edit and submit a pull request.
  • For large or code changes, fork the repo, create a branch named fix/<short-description> or feat/<short-description>, make your changes, then open a pull request against main.
  • Include a clear PR description with what you changed and why, and link any relevant issues.

docs.title: Pull request guidelines
docs.text:
• Target branch: main.
  • Use descriptive, imperative commit messages (e.g., "Fix broken command example").
  • If your change affects behavior or API, add tests or instructions for reviewers to verify.

docs.title: Editing docs locally
docs.text:
1. Create and activate a Python venv in the repository root:
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
  1. Install dependencies:
python -m pip install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel
pip install -r requirements.txt
  1. Build or serve the docs locally:
python -m mkdocs build
# or for live reload
mkdocs serve
  1. After editing Markdown files, run mkdocs build to check the site builds and review changes at http://127.0.0.1:8000 when using mkdocs serve.

docs.title: Coding style & tests
docs.text:
• Follow existing code style in the repository. Keep changes minimal and focused.
  • Run existing tests where applicable and include instructions in your PR if special steps are needed.

docs.title: Reporting issues or requesting help
docs.text:
• Open an issue at: https://github.com/devvyyxyz/xeno-bot-docs/issues
  • Join the support Discord (see the Links page) if you need live help.

docs.title: License and contributor agreement
docs.text:
By submitting a pull request you agree to the repository's license and that your contribution will be made available under the same license.

If you'd like, I can add a PR template and issue templates to this repository to make contributions smoother.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide shows how to run the docs site locally and build the static site for deployment.

docs.title: Local preview
docs.text:
Install dependencies and run the local server:

python -m pip install -r requirements.txt
mkdocs serve

Open http://127.0.0.1:8000 in your browser.

docs.title: Build
docs.text:
mkdocs build

The built site will be in the site/ directory.

docs.title: Project History
docs.text: High-level timeline and important milestones for the bot.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
This project documents the xeno-bot Node.js project. To run or develop the bot itself, follow the upstream repository's Node/npm instructions.

Clone the bot repository (example):

git clone https://github.com/devvyyxyz/xeno-bot.git
cd xeno-bot

Install Node dependencies

node --version   # Node 18+ recommended
npm install

Environment

  • Copy .env.example to .env and fill values (e.g. TOKEN, CLIENT_ID, optional GUILD_ID).
  • DATABASE_URL — optional for Postgres/MySQL; otherwise dev uses local SQLite at data/dev.sqlite.

Run commands

# start in development (nodemon):
npm run dev

# production start (sharded by default):
npm start

# deploy slash commands (use with care):
npm run deploy:public

Docs site (this repository)

The documentation site is built with MkDocs. To edit/preview these docs, see this repo's README and run the MkDocs instructions in the docs root.

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
Useful links for Xeno Bot: - GitHub: https://github.com/devvyyxyz/xeno-bot-docs - Bot vote (top.gg): https://top.gg/bot/1476427270326583306 - Support Discord server: https://discord.gg/WmFDeMKvzj - Invite the bot: https://discord.com/oauth2/authorize?client_id=1476427270326583306&scope=bot%20applications.commands&permissions=0

You can place these links in your server or add them to embedded messages and the dashboard.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Xeno Bot is a Discord bot scaffold built on discord.js v14 focused on scalability, modular commands, and extensible server-side configuration. This docs site documents the public repo at https://github.com/devvyyxyz/xeno-bot and provides onboarding, developer guidance, and architecture notes.

Key features

  • Command-driven architecture (slash & message commands)
  • Server-configurable behavior via config/ and runtime /.env
  • Database support via knex (SQLite for dev, Postgres/MySQL in production)
  • Sharding support and production-ready deployment scripts
  • Dashboard and web UI for server configuration (optional)
